title: A Streetcar Named Desire
creator: Tennessee Williams
description: A fragile woman arrives in New Orleans and disrupts her sister’s marriage.
whyForActors: Blanche DuBois is one of the most frequently performed roles in modern theatre.

title: Hamlet
creator: William Shakespeare
description: A prince returns home to grief, betrayal, and revenge.
whyForActors: The role of Hamlet is often considered one of the longest in the Shakespeare canon.

title: The Seagull
creator: Anton Chekhov
description: Artists, lovers, and families gather at a country estate.
whyForActors: Characters frequently speak around what they mean rather than directly stating it.

title: The Cherry Orchard
creator: Anton Chekhov
description: A family estate is sold as an old world begins to disappear.
whyForActors: Chekhov described the play as a comedy despite its themes of loss.

title: Death of a Salesman
creator: Arthur Miller
description: A salesman struggles with memory, expectation, and identity.
whyForActors: The structure shifts between present action and memory sequences.

title: Long Day’s Journey Into Night
creator: Eugene O’Neill
description: A family confronts addiction, resentment, and the past over the course of a day.
whyForActors: The play takes place in real time across a single day.

title: Who’s Afraid of Virginia Woolf?
creator: Edward Albee
description: Two couples meet for drinks that extend into the early hours of the morning.
whyForActors: The action unfolds continuously over one night.

title: Top Girls
creator: Caryl Churchill
description: A woman’s professional life intersects with historical and social perspectives.
whyForActors: The opening scene features characters from different historical periods together.

title: The Homecoming
creator: Harold Pinter
description: A man returns home with his wife to his all-male family.
whyForActors: Pinter’s writing is known for pauses and subtext-driven dialogue.

title: A Doll’s House
creator: Henrik Ibsen
description: A woman begins to question her role within her marriage and home.
whyForActors: The final scene has been widely discussed and reinterpreted in modern theatre.

title: The Glass Menagerie
creator: Tennessee Williams
description: A memory play about a family living in St. Louis.
whyForActors: The narrator is also a character within the events of the play.

title: Waiting for Godot
creator: Samuel Beckett
description: Two figures wait by a tree for someone who never arrives.
whyForActors: The structure repeats similar actions across both acts.

title: The Crucible
creator: Arthur Miller
description: Accusations of witchcraft spread through a Puritan community.
whyForActors: The play is often used as an allegory for political persecution.

title: Angels in America
creator: Tony Kushner
description: Multiple characters navigate identity, politics, and illness in America.
whyForActors: Actors often play multiple roles across the production.

title: Jerusalem
creator: Jez Butterworth
description: A charismatic outsider lives on the edge of a rural English community.
whyForActors: The central character appears in nearly every scene.

title: People, Places & Things
creator: Duncan Macmillan
description: An actor enters rehab and confronts addiction and identity.
whyForActors: The play explores performance both on and off stage.

title: The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Night-Time
creator: Simon Stephens
description: A boy investigates the death of a neighbour’s dog.
whyForActors: The story is told from the perspective of the central character.

title: Constellations
creator: Nick Payne
description: A relationship is explored across multiple possible realities.
whyForActors: Scenes are repeated with slight variations throughout the play.

title: The Effect
creator: Lucy Prebble
description: Two participants in a clinical trial develop a relationship.
whyForActors: Questions whether emotions are caused by chemistry or genuine feeling.

title: Yerma
creator: Federico García Lorca
description: A woman’s desire for a child shapes her life and relationships.
whyForActors: The play draws heavily on poetic and symbolic language.
